is usually done by a [sports governing body](/wiki/Sports_governing_body "Sports governing body") for each [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport"), resulting in a core of relatively invariant, agreed [rules](/wiki/Game#Rules_and_aims "Game"). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Sport rule](/w/index.php?title=Sport_rule&redirect=no "Sport rule"))

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a [list of references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), [related reading](/wiki/Wikipedia:Further_reading "Wikipedia:Further reading"), or [external links](/wiki/Wikipedia:External_links "Wikipedia:External links"), **but its sources remain unclear because it lacks [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Fact_and_Reference_Check "Wikipedia:WikiProject Fact and Reference Check") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(August 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **relies largely or entirely on a [single source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Articles_with_a_single_source "Wikipedia:Articles with a single source")**. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Regulation_of_sport "Talk:Regulation of sport"). Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Regulation_of_sport&action=edit) by [introducing citations to additional sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). *Find sources:* ["Regulation of sport"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Regulation+of+sport%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Regulation+of+sport%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Regulation+of+sport%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Regulation+of+sport%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Regulation+of+sport%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Regulation+of+sport%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2024)* |

The **regulation of sport** is usually done by a [sports governing body](/wiki/Sports_governing_body "Sports governing body") for each [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport"), resulting in a core of relatively invariant, agreed [rules](/wiki/Game#Rules_and_aims "Game"). People responsible for [leisure](/wiki/Leisure "Leisure") activities often seek recognition and respectability as sports by joining sports federations such as the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee"), or by forming their own regulatory body. In this way sports evolve from leisure activity to more formal sports: relatively recent newcomers are [BMX cycling](/wiki/BMX "BMX"), [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding"), [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), etc. Some of these activities have been popular but uncodified pursuits for different lengths of time. Indeed, the formal regulation of sport is a relatively modern and increasing development. This method promotes a sport globally, in a very successful way.

It also promotes the universality of each sport, by ensuring that the same gameplay rules are being practiced worldwide, using a standardized/homogenous international gameplay rule system (sanctioned by the respective international sports governing bodies) that is applied uniformly on all member associations and recognized leagues.

The degree of organisation can vary from national or worldwide competitions for the sport, or it can occur in a purely *ad hoc*, spontaneous way. A sport may be played individually (e.g. time trialling in cycling) or in a team, or just for recreation and well being (e.g. swimming).

Some challenging situations have had to be dealt with when there is an overlap of the regulation of the sport with other forms of regulation, e.g. safety (there have been serious losses of life in football audiences, through stand collapses or poor crowd management), or simple laws of the land (some inadvertent or otherwise physical interchanges occur between participants).

Examples are [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in association football and [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") in basketball, which have regulated international gameplay rules that are even practiced within US sports leagues today, despite not practicing them historically (which therefore meant that many US sports leagues weren't recognized by international governing bodies in the past, until they began to adopt international rules). In the sport of basketball, the defender/defense cannot call foul.

## Formula One

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regulation_of_sport&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Formula One")]

[Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") motor racing is an example of strict and changing regulation, where the regulating body appears to control rather than to simply define the sport. There have been major changes in the rules of F1 recently, almost on an annual basis, and more are planned. Sometimes this is done for safety reasons, sometimes to make the racing more interesting as a [spectator sport](/wiki/Spectator_sport "Spectator sport"), and sometimes to promote [competition](/wiki/Competition "Competition") through involvement of smaller [teams](/wiki/Team "Team"). Some changes make overtaking more probable for example or reduce the probability of an overwhelming [technical](/wiki/Technology "Technology") advantage by any one team. Although heavily regulated, most people agree that the sport has thereby greatly benefitted, not least through dramatic leaps in [safety](/wiki/Safety "Safety").

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regulation_of_sport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

The [broadcasting of sports events](/wiki/Broadcasting_of_sports_events "Broadcasting of sports events") is also highly regulated, with contracts limiting who can show footage.
